quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Static friction"?

options.text: A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: "Static friction" is defined as: f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Kinetic friction"?

options.text: Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction: ΔE_thermal = f_k × d
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s
The gravitational force W = mg directed toward Earth's center; a body force, not a contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Kinetic friction" is defined as: f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Coefficient of friction"?

options.text: ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ - θ₀) for constant angular acceleration
F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces
μ (dimensionless); characterizes the roughness between two surfaces; depends on material pair, not contact area

quiz_questions.explanation: "Coefficient of friction" is defined as: μ (dimensionless); characterizes the roughness between two surfaces; depends on material pair, not contact area. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Normal force on incline"?

options.text: KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
N = mgcosθ on a frictionless incline; modified by other forces pressing against or pulling away from the surface
The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Normal force on incline" is defined as: N = mgcosθ on a frictionless incline; modified by other forces pressing against or pulling away from the surface. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Friction as a non-conservative force"?

options.text: The gravitational force on an object is the vector sum of the gravitational forces from all other masses
Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative
When τ_net,ext = 0: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂; if I decreases, ω increases proportionally
In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision

quiz_questions.explanation: "Friction as a non-conservative force" is defined as: Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N"?

options.text: System of equations
Impulse-momentum theorem
Static friction
Tension

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Static friction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f_k = μ_k N; the friction force during sliding, proportional to the normal force; typically μ_k < μ_s"?

options.text: Power
Dot product
Newton's Second Law for rotation
Kinetic friction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Kinetic friction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "μ (dimensionless); characterizes the roughness between two surfaces; depends on material pair, not contact area"?

options.text: Center of mass motion
Coefficient of friction
Weight
Horsepower

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Coefficient of friction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "N = mgcosθ on a frictionless incline; modified by other forces pressing against or pulling away from the surface"?

options.text: Rotational kinetic energy
Normal force on incline
Impulse-momentum theorem
Torque

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Normal force on incline".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative"?

options.text: Newton's Second Law for rotation
Work-Energy Theorem
Rotational kinematics
Friction as a non-conservative force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Friction as a non-conservative force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Friction and Normal Forces is accurate?

options.text: The physical (compound) pendulum accounts for distributed mass: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)), where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot and d is the distance from pivot to center of mass.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.
Friction and Normal Forces describe contact forces that resist relative motion between surfaces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Friction and Normal Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.
Standard formulas exist for common shapes (rod, disk, sphere, hoop) that appear frequently on the AP exam.
Setting gravitational force equal to centripetal force: GMm/r² = mv²/r gives orbital speed v = √(GM/r) and period T = 2π√(r³/GM), which is Kepler's Third Law.
Static friction (f_s ≤ μ_s N) prevents motion up to a maximum value, while kinetic friction (f_k = μ_k N) acts during sliding.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Friction and Normal Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: The normal force N adjusts to maintain contact and is not always equal to mg—it depends on the geometry and other applied forces.
Power analysis is critical for engine design, electrical systems, and athletic performance.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Friction and Normal Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: SHM is the foundation for understanding waves, circuits, and quantum mechanics.
Friction problems often involve inclined planes, circular motion, and connected bodies.
Geosynchronous orbits, escape velocity (v_esc = √(2GM/r)), and energy in orbits (E = -GMm/2r) are key applications of gravitational and energy concepts.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Friction and Normal Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.
Friction and Normal Forces describe contact forces that resist relative motion between surfaces.
The impulse-momentum theorem (J = ∫F dt = Δp) connects the time integral of force to the change in momentum, making it especially useful for analyzing collisions and impacts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Friction and Normal Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: Wrench design and usage: longer handles provide greater torque for the same applied force, following τ = rF
Automotive crash testing measuring force-time profiles with accelerometers to calculate impulse and evaluate occupant protection systems
Tire engineering optimizing rubber compounds to maximize μ_s for different road conditions (dry, wet, icy) for vehicle safety
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Friction and Normal Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: Analyzing bungee jump physics using conservation of energy between gravitational PE and elastic PE to determine maximum stretch and maximum speed
SpaceX and NASA calculating orbital insertion burns using v = √(GM/r) to place satellites at specific altitudes and orbital velocities
Analyzing braking distance on inclined roads where both gravity component and friction determine deceleration
Roller coaster engineering ensuring the car speed at the top of a loop exceeds √(gr) so riders maintain contact with their seats

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Friction and Normal Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Friction and Normal Forces applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips
Nuclear physics analyzing particle collision products using conservation of momentum in two dimensions to identify unknown particles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Friction and Normal Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Friction and Normal Forces?

options.text: Interplanetary mission design computing escape velocity from Earth and transfer orbits (Hohmann transfers) to reach Mars or other planets
Tire engineering optimizing rubber compounds to maximize μ_s for different road conditions (dry, wet, icy) for vehicle safety
Quartz crystal oscillators in watches using the precise SHM frequency of a quartz crystal (32,768 Hz) for accurate timekeeping
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Friction and Normal Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Friction and Normal Forces in practice?

options.text: Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Quartz crystal oscillators in watches using the precise SHM frequency of a quartz crystal (32,768 Hz) for accurate timekeeping
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data
Analyzing braking distance on inclined roads where both gravity component and friction determine deceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Friction and Normal Forces.
